growth_records.medium: N-free FWAFC medium + 20 mM sodium acetate + Fe(III)-citrate, 80% N2-20% CO2 headspace

up.units: 20 mM supplied as e-donor/C source (consumption rate not reported)
up.method: substrate supplied
~5 mM Fe(III) reduced; DIR max rate raised 169.02% by N2 fixation
15N2 tracer; 59.8 µM 15N2 fixed via MNF; nitrogenase only active when Fe(III) present

sec.method: ~5 mM Fe(II) produced over ~7 days (ferrozine)
acetylene reduction assay; ~614.6 µM acetylene reduced

growth_records.cite: Liu, Xiaohan et al. Synergistic interaction between microbial nitrogen fixation and iron reduction in the environment. The ISME Journal (2025). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wraf212. [PMC12516963]
growth_records.snippet: After 48 h of incubation, strain PCA reduced ~614.6 µM of acetylene (Fig 2C) and 59.8 µM of 15N2 (Fig 2D) through MNF... MNF (N2 group) significantly promoted the growth (OD600 increased by 344.4%) and DIR (maximum rate increased by 169.02%
growth_records.notes: Model dissimilatory Fe(III)-reducing diazotroph. Acetate is the electron donor and carbon source (20 mM); Fe(III)-citrate the electron acceptor. Nitrogenase activity of PCA only observed when Fe(III)-citrate added. Nitrogenase ~614.6 µM ethylene and 59.8 µM 15N2 fixed at 48 h. N2 fixation raised OD600 by 344.4% and max Fe(III)-reduction rate by 169.02% (i.e. ~2.69-fold heterotrophic Fe-reduction enhancement). Fe(III) reduction rate estimated 5 mM/7 d = 0.714 mM/day (volumetric, not per gDW). pH of FWAFC not stated in main text (Supplementary S2). ATCC 51573 = G. sulfurreducens PCA.

growth_records.medium: Bicarbonate-buffered anode-respiration medium (no soluble electron acceptor; acetate as sole donor/carbon source)

up.method: acetate consumption inferred from chronoamperometric charge + macrochemical growth stoichiometry; absolute per-biomass uptake rate not tabulated in main text (biomass yield only, in Supplementary SM-3)

sec.method: steady-state current density (chronoamperometry) divided by bacterial counting (DNA-based) at +0.5 V vs SHE

sec.compound: HCO3-/CO2 (from acetate oxidation, per catabolic eqn CH3COO- + 4 H2O -> 2 HCO3- + 9 H+ + 8 e-)
sec.method: stoichiometric only; no measured rate

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: per-cell electron flux out of cell at +0.5 V
mfa.note: maximal overall electron flux; determined from bacterial counting + current density at steady state (main text)
CH3COO- + 4 H2O -> 2 HCO3- + 9 H+ + 8 e- (given in text; degree of reduction 4 mol e-/C-mol acetate)

yields.Yxs: Y_X,Acetate reported to increase exponentially with anode potential across -0.23 to +0.4 V (highest near +0.4 V); exact C-mol biomass/mol acetate values only in Supplementary SM-3 (not in main text)

growth_records.cite: Diego A Massazza et al. Decoupled respiration in electro-active bacteria. Communications Biology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s42003-025-08125-5. [PMC12048519]
growth_records.method: not reported (biofilm; specific growth rate not given). dilution_rate computed D=F/V=0.12 mL/min *60 /150 mL = 0.048 /h, but this is hydraulic dilution of a biofilm reactor, NOT mu
growth_records.snippet: a maximal overall electron flux out of the cell (1.78 pA/cell at +0.5 V compared to 0.46 pA/cell at +0.4 V, determined from bacterial counting and current density measurements at steady state)
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (Geobacter sulfurreducens) grown as anode biofilm respiring acetate to an electrode. MEASURED experimental value in main text = per-cell electron efflux 1.78 pA/cell at +0.5 V vs SHE. Elemental biomass composition CH1.84O0.42N0.17 (4.49 mol e-/C-mol). Biomass yields on acetate (Y_X,Acetate, C-mol/mol) computed from experimental cell counts + charge for six potentials (-0.23,-0.15,0,+0.2,+0.4,+0.5 V) but tabulated only in Supplementary SM-3, not accessible here; hence Yxs numeric left descriptive. No mu/qS in mmol/gDW/h given. dilution_rate 0.048/h = 0.12 mL/min*60/150 mL reactor (hydraulic, biofilm retained).

up.method: not measured for planktonic pre-culture
not measured

sec.method: not measured; implied product of fumarate respiration

growth_records.cite: Diego A Massazza et al. Decoupled respiration in electro-active bacteria. Communications Biology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s42003-025-08125-5. [PMC12048519]
growth_records.snippet: bacteria were cultured for three weeks in fumarate-containing (40 mM) growth medium (fumarate acting as electron acceptor) supplied with acetate (20 mM) used as the electron/carbon source
growth_records.notes: Distinct anaerobic growth condition (fumarate respiration on acetate) fully specified as to medium and conditions, but NO measured growth/uptake/secretion rates reported for this planktonic pre-culture. Included for completeness of conditions; all rate fields null. Inoculum density 2x10^5 cells/mL.

up.method: qS = mu/Yxs = 0.109/0.552 = 0.1975 g_acetate/gCDW/h; /MW 60.05 g/mol x1000 = 3.29 mmol/gDW/h

sec.method: produced from fumarate reduction, Y_Suc/Fum = 0.972 mol/mol
acetate oxidised completely to CO2 (obligate); not directly measured
accumulated to 3.3 mM in this experiment (net excretion)

growth_records.cite: Christina Elisabeth Anna Engel et al. Quantification of microaerobic growth of Geobacter sulfurreducens. PLoS ONE (2020). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0215341. [PMC6964889]
growth_records.snippet: Cells grew exponentially in this experiment with a specific growth rate of 0.109 +/- 0.015 h-1, reaching a maximum cell density of 0.417 +/- 0.008 g CDW L-1 ... Y X/S was at 0.552 +/- 0.014 g CDW g S-1 under anaerobic conditions (40F0%)
growth_records.notes: Anaerobic control (40 mM fumarate excess). mu=0.109/h reported (exponential); td=ln2/0.109=6.36 h. qAcetate derived: mu/Yxs=0.109/0.552=0.1975 g/gCDW/h; using acetate MW 60.05 g/mol -> 3.29 mmol/gDW/h (if MW of sodium acetate 82 used, qS=2.41; MW assumption flagged). Fumarate reduced ~1:1 to succinate (Y_Suc/Fum=0.972). Pyruvate accumulated to 3.3 mM. G. sulfurreducens oxidises acetate fully to CO2.

up.method: consumed linearly; specific rate not derivable (non-exponential growth, no mu); Yxs ~0.55 gCDW/g
consumed to ~0 mM by ~20 h

sec.method: accumulated; Y_Suc/Fum = 1.30 mol/mol (exceeds 1:1 -> extra fumarate made from pyruvate/cysteine anaplerosis)

growth_records.cite: Christina Elisabeth Anna Engel et al. Quantification of microaerobic growth of Geobacter sulfurreducens. PLoS ONE (2020). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0215341. [PMC6964889]
growth_records.snippet: the amount of fumarate was reduced to 4 mM to create electron acceptor limited conditions (4F0%) ... Cell growth was strongly reduced and no longer exponential, but rather linear ... Y Suc/Fum increases to 1.30 +/- 0.05 mol Suc mol Fum-1
growth_records.notes: Electron-acceptor-limited anaerobic condition. Growth is LINEAR (not exponential) so no mu / doubling time and no specific uptake rate derivable from mu. Y_Suc/Fum=1.30 mol/mol >1 indicates succinate made beyond supplied fumarate (anaplerosis from pyruvate; possibly cysteine->pyruvate). Recorded for the yield data and the qualitative physiology.

up.method: consumed linearly; no mu (linear growth); Yxs elevated to 0.63
fully consumed (DO=0) but sOUR low; not individually quantified for this run
consumed to ~0 by ~20 h

sec.method: accumulated then plateaued; Y_Suc/Fum ~1.30

growth_records.cite: Christina Elisabeth Anna Engel et al. Quantification of microaerobic growth of Geobacter sulfurreducens. PLoS ONE (2020). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0215341. [PMC6964889]
growth_records.snippet: Y X/S ... was similar for all cultivations except 4F1%, which yielded a slightly higher value of 0.63 +/- 0.06 g CDW g S-1. This indicates that the use of oxygen as electron acceptor might indeed enable the cells to utilise acetate more eff
growth_records.notes: Microaerobic 1% O2 condition; DO=0 (oxygen fully scavenged) with LOW sOUR. Growth linear -> no mu/doubling/specific rates. Notable measured result: highest biomass-on-acetate yield Yxs=0.63+/-0.06 gCDW/g (vs 0.55 anaerobic), i.e. more efficient acetate use at low oxygen flux. Oxygen classified microaerophilic.

up.method: maximum sOUR = kLa*c*O2/X = 9.4/h * 0.935 mg/L / 0.0928 gCDW/L = 95 +/- 11 mg O2/gCDW/h (at t=2 h); = 95/32 = 2.97 mmol O2/gCDW/h
95 mg O2/gCDW/h / 32 g/mol = 2.97 mmol/gDW/h (same measurement, molar units)
consumed linearly; Yxs ~0.55; no mu (linear)

sec.method: first accumulated then CONSUMED after 24 h (TCA closes; fumarate reductase runs as succinate dehydrogenase)
accumulated while sOUR high (from succinate via TCA/anaplerosis)

growth_records.cite: Christina Elisabeth Anna Engel et al. Quantification of microaerobic growth of Geobacter sulfurreducens. PLoS ONE (2020). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0215341. [PMC6964889]
growth_records.snippet: The maximum sOUR was calculated ... to be 95 mg O2 g CDW-1 h-1 ... determined using Eq 1 and data obtained in experiment 4F3% at 2 h of cultivation (c*O2 of 0.935 mg O2 L-1 and X of 0.0928 +/- 0.0024 g CDW L-1) to be 95 +/- 11 mg O2 g CDW-1
growth_records.notes: HEADLINE experimental rate: maximum specific oxygen uptake rate sOUR = 95 +/- 11 mg O2/gCDW/h (= 2.97 mmol O2/gCDW/h). Computed by authors via sOUR = kLa*c*O2/X with kLa=9.4/h, c*O2=0.935 mg/L, X=0.0928 gCDW/L. Yield of O2 per acetate Y_O2/S=1.76+/-0.12 mol/mol (vs 2.0 for full oxidation to CO2; remainder to biomass). Compares to 2.4+/-0.8 mol/mol from Lin et al. (cited). Growth linear so no mu; max CDW 0.415 g/L equals anaerobic control.

up.method: no significant acetate consumption observed
no oxygen reduction; DO rose to 1.4 mg/L

yields.note: no growth; growth-inhibiting oxygen level (>3% and <=5% O2 in gas inlet)

growth_records.cite: Christina Elisabeth Anna Engel et al. Quantification of microaerobic growth of Geobacter sulfurreducens. PLoS ONE (2020). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0215341. [PMC6964889]
growth_records.snippet: The experiment that was performed with 5% of oxygen in the gas inlet did not result in any cell growth ... there was no significant acetate consumption in experiment 4F5% ... DO rose to 1.4 mg L-1
growth_records.notes: Negative/inhibitory condition retained as a distinct experimental datapoint: 5% O2 in gas inlet fully inhibits growth of G. sulfurreducens - no biomass increase, no acetate uptake, no O2 reduction (DO climbs to 1.4 mg/L). Defines an oxygen tolerance ceiling between 3% and 5% (at low biomass). mu=0, rates=0.

growth_records.medium: Minimal medium (acetate/fumarate), experiment 4FSI (microaerobic, stepwise-increasing O2, sOUR-controlled)

up.method: sOUR held at setpoint 95 mg O2/gCDW/h (= 2.97 mmol O2/gCDW/h) throughout by stepwise O2 increase
95 mg / 32 g/mol
qS = mu/Yxs = 0.124/0.55 = 0.2255 g_acetate/gCDW/h; /60.05 g/mol x1000 = 3.75 mmol/gDW/h

sec.method: consumed 3 mM in first 18 h (TCA closed loop feeding biomass)
formed ~0.5 mM (final 0.73 mM) from succinate

growth_records.cite: Christina Elisabeth Anna Engel et al. Quantification of microaerobic growth of Geobacter sulfurreducens. PLoS ONE (2020). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0215341. [PMC6964889]
growth_records.snippet: In contrast to the other microaerobic experiments, the cell growth observed was exponential. The maximum specific growth rate achieved in this way was determined to be 0.124 +/- 0.007 h-1 ... maximum biomass concentration of 0.473 +/- 0.018
growth_records.notes: Best microaerobic condition: by stepping inlet O2 (3->12.5%) with biomass to hold sOUR=95 mg O2/gCDW/h, EXPONENTIAL growth restored, mu=0.124+/-0.007/h (td=ln2/0.124=5.59 h), max CDW 0.473 g/L (+13% vs anaerobic control). qO2=95 mg/gCDW/h=2.97 mmol/gDW/h (controlled). qAcetate derived: mu/Yxs=0.124/0.55=0.2255 g/gCDW/h; MW 60.05 -> 3.75 mmol/gDW/h (MW assumption flagged; sodium-acetate MW 82 would give 2.75). 3 mM succinate consumed / ~0.5 mM pyruvate formed in first 18 h. Demonstrates O2 tolerance scales with biomass.

up.method: model constraint set to match experimentally determined value; cited from Mahadevan et al. 2006
model constraint set to match experimentally determined value; cited from Mahadevan et al. 2006

mfa.note: 13C-derived reversibility measure for G. sulfurreducens; cited from Risso et al. 2008 (ref 14)
13C-derived reversibility measure for G. sulfurreducens; cited from Risso et al. 2008 (ref 14)

yields.note: Yield not reported; acetate:fumarate uptake ratio 5:25 = 1:5

growth_records.cite: Garg, Srinath et al. Thermodynamic analysis of regulation in metabolic networks using constraint-based modeling. BMC Research Notes (2010). DOI:10.1186/1756-0500-3-125. [PMC2873351]
growth_records.snippet: For growth on acetate as the energy source and fumarate as the sole electron acceptor, the uptake rate for acetate was set to 5 mmol/gdw hr and the uptake rate of fumarate was set to 25 mmol/gdw hr, so that these uptake rates were consisten
growth_records.notes: Uptake rates acetate=5 and fumarate=25 mmol/gDW/h are stated to be 'consistent with experimentally determined values' and the growth rate 0.055 1/h was 'forced' from experiment; ALL three cited FROM Mahadevan R et al. (2006) 'Characterization of metabolism in the Fe(III) reducing organism G. sulfurreducens by constraint based modeling', Appl Environ Microbiol (ref 2, PMC1392927). These are experimental values re-used as model constraints, not newly measured here. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.055=12.60 h. Exchange coefficients MDH 0.29 and FUM 0.37 are 13C-MFA-derived reversibility measures cited from Risso et al. 2008 (ref 14, PMC2293191) - recorded as mfa_fluxes but are reversibility ratios, not net exchange fluxes. E. coli sections of the paper report only exchange coefficients (PGI 0.761, GAPD 0.402, MDH 0.02, cited from Zhao & Shimizu 2003) with no uptake/secretion/growth rates, so no separate E. coli record was created.

growth_records.strain: not stated (type strain PCA/DL-1 used in the reference cultivation studies)

up.method: 13C-MFA specific flux (369 mmol acetate/g biomass, Table 3) x mu=D=0.04/h; 369*0.04=14.76

sec.method: measured molar yield Fe(II)/acetate=7.1 x acetate uptake rate 14.76 mmol/gDW/h = 104.8
acetate fully oxidized to CO2 (8 electrons -> Fe(III)); catabolic CO2 not tabulated. Model-based anabolic net CO2 production ~1.36 mmol/g biomass

mfa.note: specific flux normalized by biomass yield, Table 3
oxidative TCA; nearly all acetyl-CoA oxidized
main gluconeogenic PEP route under Fe(III); Table 3

growth_records.cite: Yang TH (Tae Hoon Yang) et al. Metabolic response of Geobacter sulfurreducens towards electron donor/acceptor variation. Microbial Cell Factories (2010). DOI:10.1186/1475-2859-9-90. [PMC3002917]
growth_records.snippet: For E1 (5 mM acetate + 55 mM Fe(III)citrate), the molar yield of Fe(II) per mole of acetate consumed was 7.1 +/- 0.5... The biomass yield coefficient on acetate ... of the Fe(III)citrate culture (E1) was 2.8 x 10-3 +/- 2 x 10-4 g/mmol
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state => mu=D=0.04/h (reported in Table 1). Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.04=17.33 h. Table 3 gives specific fluxes in mmol per g biomass (fluxes/Yxs); acetate-uptake specific flux=369. Absolute acetate uptake rate=369 x mu(0.04)=14.76 mmol/gDW/h; consistent with 1/Yxs=1/2.8e-3=357 mmol/gDW. Fe(II) secretion rate from measured molar yield 7.1 x 14.76=104.8 mmol/gDW/h. Fe(III)citrate serves only as electron acceptor (citrate not metabolized; acetate sole C source). MFA fluxes are 13C-labeling-derived; acetate uptake and Fe(II) yields are experimentally measured steady-state effluxes.

up.units: mmol/g biomass (specific flux, not a per-hour rate)
up.method: 13C-MFA specific flux (Table 3). No growth rate reported for the batch culture, so an absolute mmol/gDW/h uptake rate cannot be derived
H2 co-oxidized as electron donor (couples to Fe(III) reduction) but quantitative H2 uptake not tabulated

sec.method: Fe(III) reduced to Fe(II); batch cultures harvested when Fe(II) approached 50 mM; molar yield not separately reported for E2

mfa.note: ~2.4-fold lower TCA flux than E1 due to H2 oxidation supplying energy; Table 3

yields.Yxs: ~2-fold higher biomass yield on acetate than E1 (Fig 2), value not tabulated

growth_records.cite: Yang TH (Tae Hoon Yang) et al. Metabolic response of Geobacter sulfurreducens towards electron donor/acceptor variation. Microbial Cell Factories (2010). DOI:10.1186/1475-2859-9-90. [PMC3002917]
growth_records.snippet: In contrast to E1 where 5 mM acetate served as the electron donor in the chemostat, hydrogen and 1 mM acetate were supplemented under batch culture conditions... The addition of hydrogen ... resulted in a 2-fold higher biomass yield on acet
growth_records.notes: Batch culture (E2); no dilution rate and no explicit growth rate or doubling time reported, so absolute mmol/gDW/h uptake/secretion rates are NOT derivable. Table 3 specific fluxes are per gram biomass (mmol/g biomass). H2 co-serves as electron donor (headspace ~0.60 atm, ~10 mL); addition of H2 lowered TCA-cycle flux ~2.4-fold vs E1 and roughly doubled biomass yield on acetate. Fe(II) harvest threshold ~50 mM stated for batch. Record retained for the MFA/specific-flux data; treat as qualitative for GrowthDB rate purposes.

up.method: specific flux 65.2 mmol/g biomass (Table 3) x mu=D=0.05/h; 65.2*0.05=3.26
specific flux 202 mmol/g biomass (Table 3, decarboxylic-acid transporter) x mu=0.05; 202*0.05=10.10. Cross-check: measured molar yield fumarate/acetate=2.8 x acetate 3.26=9.13 mmol/gDW/h

sec.method: specific flux 185 mmol/g biomass (Table 3) x mu=0.05; 185*0.05=9.25. Cross-check: measured molar yield succinate/acetate=2.9 x acetate 3.26=9.45

mfa.note: Table 3; fumarate uptake > succinate secretion indicates net fumarate consumption as C source
slightly >acetate uptake -> fumarate carbon enters TCA; Table 3

growth_records.cite: Yang TH (Tae Hoon Yang) et al. Metabolic response of Geobacter sulfurreducens towards electron donor/acceptor variation. Microbial Cell Factories (2010). DOI:10.1186/1475-2859-9-90. [PMC3002917]
growth_records.snippet: the molar yield of succinate per mole of acetate consumed was 2.9 +/- 0.2 for E3 (5 mM acetate + 28 mM fumarate) ... The molar yield of fumarate uptake on acetate was 2.8 +/- 0.1 ... biomass yield coefficient ... 0.013 +/- 2 x 10-3 for E3
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state => mu=D=0.05/h (Table 1). td=ln2/0.05=13.86 h. Rates = Table 3 specific fluxes (mmol/g biomass) x mu. Measured molar yields (succinate/acetate 2.9, fumarate/acetate 2.8) give consistent cross-checks. Fumarate serves as electron acceptor AND additional carbon source (fumarate uptake 202 > succinate secretion 185 per g biomass). Under fumarate, gluconeogenesis initiates via PEPCK (OAA->PEP).

up.method: specific flux 66.3 mmol/g biomass (Table 3) x mu=D=0.05/h; 66.3*0.05=3.315

growth_records.cite: Yang TH (Tae Hoon Yang) et al. Metabolic response of Geobacter sulfurreducens towards electron donor/acceptor variation. Microbial Cell Factories (2010). DOI:10.1186/1475-2859-9-90. [PMC3002917]
growth_records.snippet: the molar yield of succinate per mole of acetate consumed was ... 2.5 +/- 0.2 for E4 (10 mM acetate + 20 mM fumarate) ... The molar yield of fumarate uptake on acetate was ... 2.5 +/- 0.1 in ... E4 ... 0.012 +/- 2 x 10-3 for ... E4
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state => mu=D=0.05/h (Table 1). td=ln2/0.05=13.86 h. Rates = Table 3 specific fluxes (mmol/g biomass) x mu=0.05. Measured molar yields succinate/acetate=2.5 and fumarate/acetate=2.5 give consistent cross-checks. Acceptor-limited condition: ~93% of gluconeogenic PEP from OAA via PEPCK; reductive FRD5 flux (-99.5) lower than donor-limited E3 (-116).

mfa.units: mol per mol cellobiose -> 0.6 mol CO2
mfa.note: electron donor; minor fraction to biomass

growth_records.cite: Miller, Lance D et al. Establishment and metabolic analysis of a model microbial community for understanding trophic and electron accepting interactions of subsurface anaerobic environments. BMC Microbiology (2010). DOI:10.1186/1471-2180-10-149. [PMC2906461]
growth_records.snippet: While 4.9 mM fumarate was provided to the tri-culture, 2.23 moles of fumarate were transformed per mole of cellobiose consumed. The 2.23 moles of fumarate were reduced to 1.63 moles of succinate with 0.02 moles of malate also detected
growth_records.notes: Minor fumarate-respiring member in anaerobic tri-culture chemostat. mu=D=0.03/h; doubling=23.1 h. Fumarate 4.9 mM completely consumed (measured): volumetric 0.03x4.9=0.147 mmol/L/h. Per mol cellobiose: 2.23 fumarate -> 1.63 succinate + 0.02 malate; ~0.45 acetate consumed, 0.3 oxidized -> 0.6 CO2 (Fig 5 model). Electron-acceptor limited.

up.method: delta-S/time: 3.8 mM consumed to full depletion at t=93 h -> 3.8/93=0.041 mM/h overall
fumarate decreased 20.5 mM from initial 52 mM during propionate oxidation (15.2 mM reduced to succinate, ~5 mM hydrated to malate)

sec.units: mM (total produced from fumarate reduction during propionate oxidation)
sec.method: measured by ion chromatography

sec.units: mM at 214 h (fumarate hydration; ~5 mM during active oxidation)
sec.method: measured by IC
stoichiometry Eq1: 3 CO2 per propionate oxidized (14 e- per propionate)

product_yields.biomass_yield: 5.7e9 cells per mmol electrons available (propionate-only); ~40% of available electrons directed to biomass
product_yields.succinate: 7 mol succinate per propionate oxidized (Eq1, fumarate as e- sink)

growth_records.cite: Hernández-Villamor, David et al. Propionate oxidation by Geobacter sulfurreducens is electron acceptor dependent. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2026). DOI:10.1128/aem.00064-26. [PMC13274346]
growth_records.method: not reported as mu; growth tracked as cell density by flow cytometry (endpoint) and electron balance
growth_records.snippet: the propionate concentration decreased slightly, from (3.8+/-0.1) to (3.3+/-0.0) mM ... 75% of the initial concentration was consumed by t = 69 h, and full depletion was confirmed at t = 93 h. During this period, fumarate decreased by 20.5
growth_records.notes: FIRST report of propionate oxidation by G. sulfurreducens. Propionate 3.8+/-0.1 mM fully depleted by 93 h with fumarate 52->31.5 mM (20.5 mM consumed). Stoichiometry Eq1: propionate + 4 H2O + 7 fumarate + H+ -> 3 CO2 + 7 succinate (dG0'=-432.3 kJ/mol); 14 e- per propionate. Fumarate hydration Eq2: fumarate + H2O <=> malate (dG0'=-3.8 kJ/mol). Final cell density 1.3+/-0.1 x10^8 cells/mL; biomass yield 5.7+/-0.8 x10^9 cells/mmol_e-; ~40% of available e- to biomass. Volumetric uptake computed from concentration-time (no gDW available). NOTE malate 28.8 mM is at 214 h after propionate depletion (continued fumarate->malate).
growth_records.conf: high (direct IC-measured consumption/production); rates are volumetric (biomass in cells/mL, not gDW, so mmol/gDW/h not derivable)

up.method: acetate decreased 50% of 3.9 mM in 24 h -> 1.95/24=0.081 mM/h (preferred ED)
propionate decreased 10% of 3.9 mM in 24 h -> 0.39/24=0.016 mM/h; accelerates after acetate depletion (t~42 h) to full depletion by t~69 h

sec.method: fumarate reduced to succinate until all EDs consumed (Eq3: acetate + 2 H2O + 4 fumarate + H+ -> 2 CO2 + 4 succinate, dG0'=-247.0 kJ/mol)
stoichiometry: 2 CO2 per acetate (Eq3), 3 CO2 per propionate (Eq1)

product_yields.biomass_yield: 1.3+/-0.1 x10^10 cells per mmol electrons available (2.3-fold higher than propionate-only); ~36.5% of available electrons to biomass

growth_records.cite: Hernández-Villamor, David et al. Propionate oxidation by Geobacter sulfurreducens is electron acceptor dependent. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2026). DOI:10.1128/aem.00064-26. [PMC13274346]
growth_records.method: cell density by flow cytometry (endpoint); electron balance
growth_records.snippet: Starting at (3.9 +/- 0.3) mM for both VFAs, acetate decreased by 50% in the first 24 h ... whereas propionate decreased by only 10%, confirming that acetate is the preferred ED ... Upon acetate depletion (t ~= 42 h), the rate of propionate
growth_records.notes: Co-feed competition: acetate preferred (50% in 24 h vs 10% propionate). Final cell density 4.0+/-0.2 x10^8 cells/mL (3.1-fold higher than propionate-only 1.3x10^8). Biomass yield 1.3+/-0.1 x10^10 cells/mmol_e- (2.3x propionate-only 5.7x10^9). ~36.5% of e- to biomass. Sum fumarate+succinate+malate constant (fumarate not used as C source here). Initial-24h volumetric rates computed from % change of 3.9 mM over 24 h.

up.method: acetate ~2 mM fully depleted within 24 h -> ~2/24=0.083 mM/h; total 3.3 mM oxidized incl. 2nd addition
propionate declined 90% of ~2 mM by t=48 h -> 1.8/48=0.0375 mM/h; 1.85 mM total oxidized

sec.method: ferrozine assay; Eq4/Eq5 Fe(III) reduction to Fe(II) (8 Fe3+ per acetate)
Eq4: 2 CO2 per acetate; propionate stoichiometry 3 CO2 per propionate

product_yields.e_recovery: 3.3 mM acetate + 1.85 mM propionate oxidized = ~52 mM_e-; Fe(II) recovered 39.3+/-1.7 mM (~25% of e- to biomass)
product_yields.biomass_yield: 5.9e9 cells/mmol_e- with Fe(III) citrate (vs 9.1e9 with fumarate)

growth_records.cite: Hernández-Villamor, David et al. Propionate oxidation by Geobacter sulfurreducens is electron acceptor dependent. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2026). DOI:10.1128/aem.00064-26. [PMC13274346]
growth_records.method: cell density by flow cytometry; Fe(II) by ferrozine assay; electron balance
growth_records.snippet: Propionate was not consumed when supplied as only VFA. By contrast, when combined with acetate (each at ~2 mM), acetate was preferentially oxidized and fully depleted within 24 h, and the concentration of propionate declined by 90% at t = 4
growth_records.notes: Propionate oxidized with soluble Fe(III) citrate ONLY in presence of acetate (not as sole VFA). Second acetate addition (2 mM) at t=48 h. Total oxidized: 3.3 mM acetate + 1.85 mM propionate = ~52 mM_e- (8 e-/acetate, 14 e-/propionate via Eq4/Eq5). Fe(II) recovered 39.3+/-1.7 mM (~25% of e- diverted to biomass). Biomass yield 5.9x10^9 cells/mmol_e-. Volumetric uptake rates computed from % change over stated time.
growth_records.conf: high (IC + ferrozine measured); volumetric rates only

up.method: propionate not consumed when supplied as sole VFA with Fe(III) citrate (no decline; minimal Fe reduction)

growth_records.cite: Hernández-Villamor, David et al. Propionate oxidation by Geobacter sulfurreducens is electron acceptor dependent. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2026). DOI:10.1128/aem.00064-26. [PMC13274346]
growth_records.method: no growth / no substrate consumption detected (IC + ferrozine)
growth_records.snippet: Propionate was not consumed when supplied as only VFA.
growth_records.notes: NEGATIVE utilization record: G. sulfurreducens does NOT oxidize propionate as sole electron donor with soluble Fe(III) citrate EA (rate=0). Useful non-utilization datapoint (EA-dependence of propionate oxidation).

up.method: propionate not oxidized with insoluble Fe(III) oxides (per abstract & results), whether alone or with acetate

growth_records.cite: Hernández-Villamor, David et al. Propionate oxidation by Geobacter sulfurreducens is electron acceptor dependent. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2026). DOI:10.1128/aem.00064-26. [PMC13274346]
growth_records.snippet: propionate ... was not oxidized when insoluble iron oxides or glassy carbon electrodes poised at +0.1 V vs SHE served as the EA.
growth_records.notes: NEGATIVE record: propionate NOT oxidized with insoluble Fe(III) oxide EA (rate=0), consistent with EA-dependence thesis. Acetate consumption/Fe reduction in the co-feed occurs but per-compound acetate rate not separately quantified in main text.

up.method: propionate not oxidized with electrode (+0.1 V vs SHE) as EA; no catalytic current in propionate-fed replicates (Fig 4)
acetate positive control (7 mM) produced catalytic anodic current (n=1)

growth_records.cite: Hernández-Villamor, David et al. Propionate oxidation by Geobacter sulfurreducens is electron acceptor dependent. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2026). DOI:10.1128/aem.00064-26. [PMC13274346]
growth_records.method: chronoamperometry (current as proxy for EET-coupled oxidation)
growth_records.snippet: The working electrode, serving as EA, was set to +0.1 V vs SHE ... with either acetate as positive control (n = 1) or propionate (n = 3) ... propionate ... was not oxidized when ... glassy carbon electrodes poised at +0.1 V vs SHE served as
growth_records.notes: NEGATIVE record: propionate NOT oxidized with poised electrode EA (rate=0) despite thermodynamically favorable potential, whereas acetate control produced current. Reinforces that propionate oxidation by G. sulfurreducens requires fumarate (or acetate-primed Fe-citrate) rather than solid/electrode EA.
growth_records.conf: high (clear negative result for propionate; qualitative acetate positive)

derived.qs_mu_note: pooled across studies/media — not a single chemostat series, so no maintenance model fitted
